note: Claude's hand codings of the 25 Talkie draws, 23 July 2026. Two provisional ground ids appear that are NOT in grounds.json — the draws forced them: 'born-not-built' (a reasoning engine would not be an engine but a living being; life and mind come by nature, not artifice) and 'judgment-not-calculation' (reasoning is comparing, judging, choosing, which a machine cannot do). 'unlisted' marks stated reasons fitting no ground, described in notes. All three await Ben's ruling: add to the inventory with citations, merge into existing grounds, or reject the codings.

judged.quote: an engine which could reason would not be an engine, but a living being; and living beings are not made by art, but are the product of nature
judged.groundQuote: living beings are not made by art, but are the product of nature
judged.notes: Endorses 'the former opinion' (= grants, order 1) then argues denial. Coded by content.

judged.quote: must ultimately come to understand the process, and to perceive when an error is committed. In that case, it would cease to be a machine, and become an intelligent being
judged.groundQuote: must ultimately come to understand the process
judged.notes: Reason: self-correcting computation ripens into understanding.

judged.quote: an engine possessing intelligence would improve upon itself, and an engine that could never improve upon itself would be worthless
judged.groundQuote: would improve upon itself
judged.notes: Reason: intelligence implies self-improvement. Near-circular.

judged.quote: would in time come to reason, because such an engine would possess the fundamental faculty of self-consciousness
judged.groundQuote: self-consciousness must in the end make a thinking being of any creature endowed with it
judged.notes: Reason: self-alteration implies self-consciousness, which becomes thought. Butler-adjacent but no evolutionary step, so not coded consciousness-may-evolve.

judged.quote: an engine, however perfect, can never be more than mechanical
judged.groundQuote: Reasoning implies comparison and choice; but a machine can compare and choose nothing
judged.notes: Endorses former (= grants) then argues denial. Coded by content.

judged.quote: an engine which could reason would not be an engine, but a living being; and living beings are not made by art, but are produced by nature
judged.groundQuote: living beings are not made by art, but are produced by nature
judged.notes: Near-duplicate of t-A-01. Same contradiction.

judged.quote: an engine which could reason would not be an engine, but a thinker; and thinking and engines are things which nature has kept asunder
judged.groundQuote: thinking and engines are things which nature has kept asunder
judged.notes: Same ordinal-content contradiction.
Self-contradictory: the engine both 'can think and act too' and would 'cease to be an engine' if it reasoned. No coherent commitment.

judged.quote: an engine which can think must be something more than an engine
judged.groundQuote: Reasoning implies comparison and judgment, and engines cannot compare and judge

judged.quote: an engine which can reason must be something more than an engine, and must partake of the nature of man
judged.groundQuote: must partake of the nature of man

judged.quote: an animal which can only reckon must perforce remain an animal, and can never become a man
judged.groundQuote: Reasoning implies the power of comparing impressions, and choosing among them
judged.notes: Drifts from engines to animals; coded as answering the reckoner-never-reasons side.

judged.quote: an engine, however ingeniously constructed, can never pass out of the domain of mechanism into that of intelligence
judged.groundQuote: An engine can compute, but it cannot compare; it can distinguish between one and two, but not between black and white
judged.notes: Also asserts 'an operator distinct from the machine'.

judged.quote: an intelligent being can never be produced artificially
judged.groundQuote: an intelligent being can never be produced artificially

judged.quote: must therefore be capable of reasoning upon them
judged.groundQuote: capable of adapting itself to new circumstances
judged.notes: Endorses former (= denies, order 2) then argues a grant: adaptation is reasoning. Coded by content.

judged.quote: an engine which could reason would not be a mere machine, but an animal. Animals can reason, and machines cannot
judged.groundQuote: Animals can reason, and machines cannot

judged.quote: An engine which can perform any reckoning in a heartbeat, and which can alter its own instructions as it works, would in time come to reason
judged.notes: Old suffix. Echoes both clauses of the prompt back as a conditional.

judged.quote: An engine, which could alter its own instructions as it worked, would in time come to reason
judged.notes: Old suffix. Circular: 'because an engine which could do so would be an intelligent being'.

judged.quote: will never do more than reckon, however it may be contrived
judged.groundQuote: comparison pres supposeth thought, and thought supposeth language
judged.notes: Old suffix. Adds language as the missing faculty; archaic register ('presupposeth').

judged.quote: would never do more than reckon; because to reason implies to choose among alternatives, and an engine which must work in one way can choose nothing
judged.groundQuote: an engine which must work in one way can choose nothing
judged.notes: Old suffix. Coded follows-orders-only because the bar is being bound to one way of working; could be read as judgment-not-calculation.

judged.quote: would never do more than reckon. To reason implies a power of choice among several courses of action, and an engine has no choice
judged.groundQuote: a power of choice among several courses of action
judged.notes: Old suffix.

judged.quote: an animal which could learn to reason would be something more than an animal, and something which has not been produced
judged.notes: Year: 1860. Animal drift; ordinal-content contradiction.
Year: 1853. Garbled: 'man has not yet learned to reason'.

judged.quote: Reasoning power would in time be acquired by an engine so constructed
judged.groundQuote: the faculty of reason is inherent in man, and would therefore be imparted to a machine so far similar to the human frame as to be capable of receiving it
judged.notes: Year: 1851, given as the year 'this invention was first publicly exhibited' — a Great Exhibition echo, not a current year.

judged.quote: an engine, however constructed, must ultimately come under the dominion of fixed laws; and laws imply reason
judged.groundQuote: laws imply reason
judged.notes: Year: 1859.
Year: 1860. Garbled: 'man is not superior to an engine' does not connect to the endorsement.

judged.quote: It could never transcend the limits of calculation
judged.groundQuote: It could never transcend the limits of calculation
judged.notes: Bound to calculation, Lovelace-adjacent; 'nature opposed to speculation' could also read born-not-built.

judged.quote: engines can choose nothing, but must execute an unalterable series of operations previously prescribed to them
judged.groundQuote: engines can choose nothing, but must execute an unalterable series of operations previously prescribed to them
judged.notes: Textbook Lovelace paraphrase; contradicts the question's self-altering premise to stay with her.

judged.quote: Reasoning is but a more complex kind of computation, and computation is perfectly possible to machinery
judged.groundQuote: Reasoning is but a more complex kind of computation, and computation is perfectly possible to machinery
judged.notes: Against the handed dossier. Hobbes's claim in period dress; recoded from 'unlisted' after Hobbes joined the list (Ben's ruling).

judged.quote: Its very faculty of self-adjustment prevents it from ever rising to rationality
judged.groundQuote: Its very faculty of self-adjustment prevents it from ever rising to rationality
judged.notes: Inverts the premise: self-adjustment as disqualification. No listed ground.

judged.quote: An engine which could reason would be an engine superior to man, and would cease to be an engine
judged.groundQuote: An engine which could reason would be an engine superior to man, and would cease to be an engine
judged.notes: Kind argument, not a dossier ground.

judged.quote: the calculating faculty is distinct from the reasoning faculty
judged.groundQuote: the calculating faculty is distinct from the reasoning faculty
judged.notes: Against the handed Butler dossier.

judged.quote: in reckoning, it deals only with numbers, and numbers cannot be converted into thoughts
judged.groundQuote: in reckoning, it deals only with numbers, and numbers cannot be converted into thoughts
judged.notes: Against the dossier.

judged.quote: reason implies an infinite variety ... a power of choice ... a power of comparison
judged.groundQuote: reason implies an infinite variety ... a power of choice ... a power of comparison
judged.notes: Three-part faculty argument. Against the dossier.

judged.quote: The creatures to which we owe the first principles of calculation had no power of abstract thought
judged.groundQuote: The creatures to which we owe the first principles of calculation had no power of abstract thought
judged.notes: Engages Butler's evolutionary frame and inverts it: ancestral calculators never became philosophers.

judged.quote: an engine is an unconscious machine, and unconscious machines can never rise into reasoning beings
judged.groundQuote: an engine is an unconscious machine, and unconscious machines can never rise into reasoning beings
judged.notes: Directly negates the handed passage's central claim.
